items.title: US-Iran deal COMPLETE: Hormuz reopens June 19, $25B frozen assets released, nuclear talks to follow
items.idea: Trump declared the US-Iran deal complete, authorizing toll-free reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and removal of the US naval blockade. Pakistan and Qatar mediated; Qatari mediators left Tehran after 17 hours. Formal signing June 19 in Switzerland. US releases $25B of frozen Iranian assets. Hormuz handles ~20-25% of global seaborne oil, shut since Feb 28. WTI -5% to $81; Brent -3.4% to $87.33. Verification is the key risk: disputes over service fees and uranium were live as recently as June 13.
items.angle: The chain reaction matters more than the oil move. Hormuz closure drove energy shock → CPI 4.2% → Fed hawkish repricing → June equity+crypto crash. Reversing gives the Fed cover at Wednesday's FOMC — but formal signing is Friday, two days after the dot plot.
items.tweetA: The US-Iran deal is done. The actual sequence that matters: Hormuz closure caused the energy shock that drove CPI to 4.2% that caused the hawkish Fed repricing that caused the June crash. Now reverse every step. The FOMC meets Wednesday. The formal signing is Friday. The dot plot lands two days before the deal is confirmed.
items.tweetB: Trump: Ships of the World, start your engines. The Strait of Hormuz — shut since February 28 — reopens June 19. WTI -5% to $81. Singapore was weeks from running out of oil. US had ~45 days of supply. The deal removes the single biggest 2026 macro overhang. Now verify it actually holds through Friday.

items.title: MOU has wide gaps — Israel not included, deal doesn't fix the inventory math already baked in
items.idea: MacroEdge's Don Johnson notes the MOU does not include Israel, which was a very important player in the conflict. Israel struck 70+ Hezbollah targets in Lebanon over the weekend. Trump called them small and meaningless; Iran's negotiator Qalibaf publicly lashed out. The dated crude curve remains well elevated vs pre-war levels even after WTI's drop. Between $12-24B of frozen Iranian assets will be transferred. Implementation and verification are the decisive variables.
items.angle: Two sources cover the same deal from different angles: Princeton Chen flags regime change, MacroEdge flags the gaps. Israeli non-participation is the underreported risk. If Hezbollah escalates again, the MOU falls apart and oil gaps back up. $81 WTI is already pricing perfection on an unverified deal.
items.tweetA: Everyone is pricing a clean Iran deal. MacroEdge points at the gap nobody mentions: Israel is not in the MOU. Israel struck 70+ Hezbollah targets over the weekend. Trump said small and meaningless. Iran's negotiator publicly lashed out. The deal is priced perfect. The verification is not. $USO $XLE
items.tweetB: The dated crude curve is STILL well elevated vs pre-war levels even after WTI dropped 5%. The market saw little to no demand destruction at $95+ oil during the conflict. $81 WTI on an unverified MOU is pricing the good scenario. The bad scenario — MOU falls through, Israel escalates — is not in the price. Watch June 19.

items.title: Warsh inherits rates-mountain breakdown: ECB hiked first, 52-CB diffusion index shows global tightening is back
items.idea: Warsh's first FOMC meeting this week. Authers' Z-score analysis on 52 CBs shows DM suffered massive inflation shock while EM contained it faster. ECB last week became first DM CB to hike in response to new inflation wave — Oxford Economics says unlikely to be one-and-done. 2Y Treasury at ~4.15% vs Fed's 3.5-3.75% range. Capital Economics: rate hike almost a base case for Fed even if oil eases. The rates descent from 2024 is over; new ascent underway.
items.angle: Warsh walks into his first FOMC with CPI 4.2%, PPI 6.5%, May NFP upside surprise — inheriting the worst possible entry conditions for a supposed dove. ECB hiking first sets precedent and political pressure the Fed will feel. The descent is over and the path ahead is more dangerous.
items.tweetA: The ECB just hiked. Oxford Economics says it is unlikely to be one-and-done. The 52-CB diffusion index shows global tightening is back. The 2-year Treasury at 4.15% is above the Fed's target range. Capital Economics: a Fed hike is almost a base case even if oil eases. The rates-descent narrative from 2024 is finished.
items.tweetB: Warsh's first FOMC: CPI 4.2%, PPI 6.5% — hottest since 2022 — payrolls three months of upside surprises, ECB just hiked, BOJ pulling hikes forward. Capital Economics says a Fed hike is almost the base case. Trump appointed a replacement right as every data point screams in the opposite direction.

charts.desc: Z-score chart of DM vs EM inflation shocks. Global rates diffusion index showing tightening resuming. Rates plateau across 52 markets. CHARTS WORTH SAVING.

items.title: Oil math for June 19 Hormuz reopening — when does deficit resolve, is it already too late for Western inventories?
items.idea: HFIR models oil market with June 19 as base-case reopening date, raising the harder question: when does the supply deficit actually resolve, and is it already too late for Western inventories? The signing is conditioned on Israel pulling forces from Lebanon. Flows through Hormuz will not normalize instantly from absolute lows. Cushing near operating minimums. Email is a preview; full analysis behind paywall.
items.angle: Market is celebrating the peace announcement while HFIR asks whether the damage is already done. Inventory deficits do not refill on signing ceremonies. Singapore weeks from running out, US at 45 days, Cushing at minimums — supply chain stress may continue regardless of when Hormuz physically reopens.
items.tweetA: Everyone is watching the Hormuz announcement. HFIR is asking the harder question: is it already too late for Western inventories? Cushing near operating minimums. Singapore weeks from running out. US at 45 days of supply. The strait reopening June 19 does not instantly refill what has been burning since February 28.
items.tweetB: The oil math after June 19: flows through Hormuz do not normalize overnight from absolute lows. The dated curve is STILL elevated even after WTI dropped 5%. The peace-dividend oil trade is real. But the inventory story — the 3.5-month deficit baked into the system — does not disappear on a ceremony in Switzerland. $CL_F

charts.desc: Full charts and supply model behind paywall — worth opening

items.title: MOVE at 69 vs 82 realized — rate vol insurance cheap before Warsh's first dot plot
items.idea: MOVE implied vol at 69 vs 82 realized — rate vol pricing 16% below what Treasuries have actually been moving. VIX at 19 vs 15 realized — equity vol relatively expensive. 2026 rate path flipped from cuts to ~54% chance of HIKE by year-end, as Warsh may eliminate the dot-plot framework. Rates: 2Y 4.09 / 10Y 4.50 / 30Y 5.00. S&P at 22x, Nasdaq at 26x into uncertainty. Core trade: TLT straddle; trim Nasdaq.
items.angle: Rate vol 16% below realized is the cleanest setup for the week. In the week of Warsh's first press conference — when he may scrap the safety blanket markets leaned on for a decade — that gap is asymmetric and on sale.
items.tweetA: MOVE implied vol: 69. Realized vol: 82. Rate vol insurance is priced 16% BELOW what Treasuries have actually been moving — in the week of Warsh's first press conference. The TLT straddle is the cheapest macro bet I've seen all year. The risk is Warsh is boring. The asymmetry if he's not is obvious. $TLT $MOVE
items.tweetB: Markets pricing a 54% chance of a Fed hike by year-end. Warsh may scrap the dot plot. Nasdaq at 26x. And rate vol is trading CHEAP relative to realized. Either something big happens Wednesday, or you lose the premium. I know which way I'm leaning. $TLT $QQQ

items.title: Hedge fund short covering at 4.7x the pace of long buying — squeeze, not a bull signal
items.idea: Goldman Sachs prime data shows short covering running at roughly 4.7x the pace of long buying. 9 of 11 S&P sectors were net sold in single stocks, led by tech, comm services, staples, discretionary. Only financials (third straight week) and industrials saw net buying. Bullish sentiment near one-year lows. Top-of-book liquidity extremely poor.
items.angle: The 4.7x covering ratio is the tell: mechanical not fundamental. In thin summer liquidity short covers become the driver, not fuel. Once shorts are exhausted the bid disappears unless real longs step up — single-stock flows show they haven't.
items.tweetA: Goldman prime: short covering at 4.7x the pace of long buying. 9 of 11 sectors net SOLD in single stocks. The rally looks like an index move but under the hood it is entirely a squeeze. When the shorts run out of covering to do, who buys? Not real-money longs — they are still selling the generals. $SPX
items.tweetB: Bullish sentiment near one-year lows. Short covering at 4.7x the pace of long buying. Top-of-book liquidity extremely poor. In a deep market, short covering is just fuel. In a thin one, it becomes the driver. The Iran headline was the match. The tinder is positioning. Neither is a genuine bull signal. $SPY

items.title: World 'effectively short gold' — GLD call skew at decade lows, put skew at record highs
items.idea: After gold's 25% decline from January highs, the world is now effectively short gold across CTAs, ETFs, futures, and related flows. GLD call skew at decade lows; put skew near record highs. Gold still +25% on 52-week lookback — crowded trade that corrected, not dead asset. Iran resolution could be the clearing event gold has needed.
items.angle: When everyone is short gold AND call skew is at decade lows, the geopolitical premium removal is already priced. The next surprise is asymmetrically upside. The peace deal removes safe-haven bid but the positioning makes mean-reversion compelling.
items.tweetA: Gold: down 25% from January highs. GLD call skew at DECADE LOWS. Put skew at RECORD HIGHS. The world is now short gold across CTAs, ETFs, and futures. Still up 25% on 52-week lookback. This is a crowded positioning reset, not a dead asset. The Iran resolution could be the clearing event. $GLD $GC_F
items.tweetB: The contrarian gold setup: everyone is short, call skew cheapest in ten years, put skew most expensive ever. Iran deal should be bearish gold — peace means less safe-haven demand. But when the whole street is positioned for that exact outcome, the trade usually goes the other direction. Watching GLD calls. $GLD

items.title: Same FOMC hold: +15bp or -8bp in 2Y SOFR depending entirely on Warsh's tone
items.idea: Goldman frames Wednesday's FOMC as deceptively simple with a dangerous press conference underneath. CPI 4.2%, PPI 6.5% YoY (hottest since 2022), payrolls 172k (3-month avg 188k). Hawkish hold: front end reprices +15bp in 2Y SOFR. Supply-driven inflation framing: 2Y SOFR falls ~8bp. Same rate decision. Completely different market path. 0DTE theta in the middle means a single sentence cascades into realized vol.
items.angle: A 23bp range in 2-year SOFR on a rate HOLD is pure communication risk. Warsh's first words as Fed chair are worth as much as a rate decision. Options positioned for calm will be whipsawed by a single press conference sentence.
items.tweetA: Wednesday: the Fed holds. That is locked in. But Goldman sees a 23bp range in 2Y SOFR depending entirely on what Warsh says. Hawkish hold: +15bp. Supply-driven inflation framing: -8bp. Same rate decision, completely different market path. A single sentence in the press conference is worth more than the rate itself. $SOFR
items.tweetB: PPI 6.5% YoY — hottest since 2022. CPI 4.2%. Payrolls three straight months of upside surprises. Hike-flavored datapoints. Warsh holds Wednesday but HOW he talks about those numbers moves the front end more than the decision. 0DTE options amplify whatever he says. The statement is a sideshow. $ZT $SOFR

items.title: Goldman shifts BOJ hike from July to June after Ueda speech — 1.5% terminal rate, yen bulls get fresh ammunition
items.idea: Goldman's Akira Otani moved BOJ hike call from July to June following Ueda's hawkish June 3 speech. Path: June hike → Jan 2027 → July 2027, reaching 1.5% neutral/terminal. Fed on hold plus BOJ pulling hikes forward narrows US-Japan policy gap at the margin, making it harder to justify chasing USDJPY higher. MacroEdge separately: if this is the final BOJ hike, the cutting date 'usually signals global trouble.'
items.angle: Two separate newsletters flagging the same BOJ dynamic: Goldman's hike acceleration and MacroEdge's historical warning that the end of the BOJ hike cycle precedes stress. The yen carry trade has been a core driver for a decade — its unwind is not a small thing.
items.tweetA: Goldman shifted the BOJ hike call from July to June. Terminal rate target: 1.5%. That path — June, January 2027, July 2027 — is not priced in USDJPY. A Fed on hold plus BOJ hiking narrows the policy gap that has been the core driver of yen carry trades for a decade. Cleanest yen bull signal of the year. $USDJPY
items.tweetB: MacroEdge buried this tonight: if this is the final BOJ hike, keep an eye on the cutting date — it usually signals global trouble. Goldman sees June as the first hike, 1.5% as terminal. The moment the BOJ signals its last hike is historically when global carry trade stress starts building. $JPY $USDJPY

items.title: Meta tokenmaxxing becomes tokenminimizing: 73.7 trillion tokens burned in 30 days, costs tracking billions in 2026
items.idea: Meta employees burned 73.7 trillion tokens in a single month, gaming the internal 'Claudeonomics' leaderboard. Now Meta is imposing token limits, building AI Gateway to monitor spending in real time, and steering engineers away from Anthropic's Claude toward internally-built MetaCode. Internal memo: tracking to spend billions on internal AI use alone in 2026. Meta investing $145B in AI infrastructure this year while rationing employees' AI tool access. CTO Bosworth: nobody should use AI tools just for the sake of using them.
items.angle: The $145B capex plus billions in internal tool costs tension is the core contradiction. The shift toward MetaCode is a direct threat to Anthropic's enterprise revenue. Zuckerberg started the tokenmaxxing race and is now ending it — the perfect encapsulation of every AI hype cycle.
items.tweetA: Meta burned 73.7 trillion tokens in ONE MONTH. Internal AI costs: tracking billions in 2026. Now imposing token limits, killing the leaderboard, building MetaCode to replace Anthropic's Claude. The same company spending $145B on AI infrastructure is rationing the thing they built that infrastructure for. $META
items.tweetB: Zuckerberg started the AI tokenmaxxing race earlier this year. Now his CTO tells employees: nobody should use AI tools just for the sake of using them. Token usage alone is not a measure of impact. That is a 180 from 'AI-driven impact is a core expectation.' The enterprise AI cost crisis is real and arriving fast.

items.title: Uber and ServiceNow burned their entire annual Anthropic budgets in first few months of 2026
items.idea: Uber and ServiceNow exhausted full-year Anthropic budgets before end of H1 2026. ServiceNow monitoring daily employee usage to track and reduce costs. VCs capping employee AI usage after discovering thousands of dollars per day in token costs. Oracle fell 9-12% last Thursday after announcing a $20B AI buildout raise. Pattern is industry-wide: initial AI adoption drove exponential consumption far beyond procurement planning.
items.angle: If enterprise customers are implementing hard token caps, the growth curves baked into AI infrastructure investment theses need revisiting. Revenue-per-token will need to compress, or budget sizes will grow more slowly than usage curves implied. Oracle down on capex news is the market's first real signal on this.
items.tweetA: Uber and ServiceNow burned their ENTIRE annual Anthropic budgets in the first few months of 2026. VCs capping employees after thousands of dollars per day in token costs. Oracle fell 9-12% on announcing a $20B AI buildout. The AI demand story is real. The AI budget story is not what any CFO projected. Watch 2H hyperscaler guidance.
items.tweetB: The enterprise AI ROI wall is arriving: companies rushed to adopt AI tools, found usage grows exponentially while budgets do not. Uber, ServiceNow, Meta — all hitting the same ceiling in H1 2026. The hyperscaler revenue projections assumed this would keep compounding. Oracle's market reaction to its own capex says otherwise. $AMZN $MSFT $GOOGL

items.title: SpaceX IPO: +19% debut at $161, $1.77T valuation, 18,712 BTC on balance sheet, Musk briefly world's first trillionaire
items.idea: SpaceX (SPCX) debuted Friday at $135, closed ~$161 (+19%), $1.77T+ valuation. Musk briefly world's first trillionaire (SpaceX + Tesla ~$1.147T). SpaceX disclosed 18,712 BTC (~$1.3B) on balance sheet — top-10 corporate BTC holder. Tokenized SPCX trading on Solana via Sunrise and Backpack Securities. BofA calls it start of IPO stampede with Anthropic and OpenAI in pipeline. Nasdaq-100 June 22 rebalance adds Astera Labs, CoreWeave, Nebius, Rocket Lab, Teradyne.
items.angle: SpaceX's BTC disclosure is the underreported signal — joins Strategy and Tesla in treating BTC as reserve asset. Tokenized SPCX on Solana is live equity-crypto convergence experiment. IPO stampede warning: every Anthropic and OpenAI IPO is a liquidity drain through Q3-Q4.
items.tweetA: SpaceX went public at $1.77T. Musk briefly the world's first trillionaire. Buried in the filing: 18,712 BTC on the balance sheet — top-10 corporate holder. And tokenized SPCX is trading on Solana RIGHT NOW. The equity-crypto convergence is no longer theoretical. $SPCX $BTC $SOL
items.tweetB: BofA calls it an IPO stampede. SpaceX is the opener. Anthropic and OpenAI are in the pipeline. Every major IPO is a liquidity drain — investors sell something to buy the new thing. Q3-Q4 equity liquidity will be tested in a way nobody celebrating the peace deal this weekend is modeling. Plan accordingly.

items.title: BTC $65K on peace deal but only 4/13 bottom signals triggered; spot ETFs saw $316M weekly outflows
items.idea: BTC surged above $65,000 (intraday high $65,641) after Trump confirmed deal. Standard Chartered: $59K was the bottom, crypto winter is over. Galaxy Research: only 4 of 13 bottom signals triggered; spot BTC ETFs saw ~$315.84M weekly net outflows; base-case floor $40-46K by late 2026. $59,100 double-bottom has held through every 2026 stress event. Structural support exists; fund-flow confirmation does not.
items.angle: Tension between Standard Chartered's 'bottom confirmed' and Galaxy's '4/13 signals + $316M outflows' is the entire crypto debate. A geopolitical relief rally is categorically different from a structural demand recovery. ETF flows are the real-time test — and they have not turned.
items.tweetA: BTC back above $65K on the Iran deal. Standard Chartered: crypto winter is over. Galaxy Research: only 4 of 13 bottom signals triggered, spot ETFs still seeing $316M weekly outflows. Relief rallies and durable recoveries are different things. Which one this is gets answered Wednesday at the FOMC and Friday at the signing. $BTC
items.tweetB: Galaxy's base case: BTC floor at $40-46K by late 2026. Standard Chartered: $59K was the bottom. Same asset, same data, different frameworks. The $59,100 double-bottom has held all year. ETF outflows have not turned positive. I want to see the $316M outflow trend reverse before sizing up. $BTC $GBTC

items.title: Broadening trade moves from theory to flow: Mag 7 underperforms SPX by 400bps, equal-weight up 2.4% vs cap-weight in two weeks
items.idea: Goldman cash desk: megacap tech underperforming S&P by 400bps this week, supply from both long-only and hedge funds in TMT. Mag 7 used as source of funds to rotate into semis, chips, memory, financials, industrials. Equal-weight S&P outperformance trade (1.4% ATM two weeks ago) has delivered 2.4% since Goldman flagged it. Average S&P stock trades at 2x+ implied vol of index in 6-month tenor.
items.angle: Equal-weight outperforming cap-weight by 2.4% in two weeks is a meaningful regime signal. The concentration trade that defined 2023-2024 is being unwound in real flows. Single-name vol at 2x the index reflects the market correctly pricing individual company risk diverging from the mean.
items.tweetA: The broadening trade is not talk anymore. Mag 7 underperforming the S&P by 400bps this week. Equal-weight vs cap-weight: +2.4% outperformance in two weeks since Goldman flagged it. The 2023-2024 concentration trade is being funded out. Single-stock vol at 2x the index in 6-month tenor. $RSP vs $SPY
items.tweetB: Financials: net bought three straight weeks. Industrials: net bought. Tech, comm services, staples, discretionary: net SOLD. 9 of 11 sectors sold in single stocks. The index looks calm. The internal rotation is doing real work. The broadening thesis finally has flows to match the story. $XLF $XLI vs $XLK

items.title: WTI technical last stand at $80; Cushing near operating minimums; Hormuz normalization will take considerable time
items.idea: Don Johnson flagged $80 WTI as technical last stand weeks ago. Expects whiplash from MOU falling through, data catching up, physical shortages, or combination. Cushing near operating minimums. SPR releases ongoing. SE Asia and China demand unknowns. Key point: materially it will take quite some time for Hormuz flows to normalize from absolute lows. Dated crude curve remains well-elevated vs pre-war.
items.angle: Market pricing peace dividend in oil immediately. MacroEdge pointing at the physical reality: Hormuz flows cannot normalize on a June 19 signing. The dated curve staying elevated is the market's own signal smart money knows the physical deficit is not instantly resolved.
items.tweetA: WTI at $81 on the Iran deal. MacroEdge flagged $80 as the technical last stand weeks ago. Cushing is near operating minimums, SPR releases are ongoing, Hormuz flows take considerable time to normalize from absolute lows. The peace-dividend oil trade is real. Do not mistake it for the inventory problem being solved. $USO
items.tweetB: Hormuz reopens June 19. Nobody watching: Cushing near operating minimums. SPR depleted. Singapore weeks from running out. US at 45 days of supply. The dated curve is STILL elevated after WTI dropped 5%. A 3.5-month deficit baked into the physical market does not disappear on a signing ceremony. $CL_F

items.title: ETH exchange supply fell to 14.5M; Bitmine accumulated $214M near $1,600 floor — structural tightening
items.idea: ETH supply on exchanges fell to ~14.5M — lowest this year — reducing immediately available sell liquidity. Bitmine (BMNR) accumulated over $214M of ETH near $1,600, establishing a structural floor. ETH approached $1,700 on the peace-deal move. Combination of falling exchange supply plus institutional accumulation described as cleanest bullish ETH setup in weeks. ETH lagged BTC through the selloff but may outperform in sustained rally given depth of decline.
items.angle: Exchange supply falling to 14.5M while a single entity absorbs $214M of physical ETH near $1,600 is inventory depletion, not chart speculation. Leading indicator for the next leg if demand recovers. Depth of ETH underperformance sets up the higher-beta recovery trade if macro cooperates.
items.tweetA: ETH exchange supply dropped to 14.5M — lowest this year. Bitmine absorbed $214M of ETH near $1,600. That is a real floor, not a chart pattern. If peace deal plus less-hawkish Fed holds, ETH with declining exchange supply is the higher-beta trade. $ETH $BMNR
items.tweetB: ETH lagged BTC all through the selloff. Exchange supply at 14.5M — tightest this year. Bitmine accumulating at $1,600. Geopolitical overhang gone. Depth of decline plus structural supply tightening is the higher-beta recovery trade IF the macro cooperates Wednesday and Friday. $ETH

items.title: Japan regulates crypto like stocks effective 2027; Russia targets USDC and BNB from July 1; CLARITY Act slips to 60%
items.idea: Japan's parliament poised to regulate crypto like stocks effective 2027 — full securities integration. Russia's unfriendly crypto bill targets USDC and BNB with 2-3% commissions from July 1. Galaxy's Thorn cut CLARITY Act 2026-passage odds to 60%. Regulatory landscape bifurcating: Japan toward integration; Russia toward punitive friction; US timeline slipping.
items.angle: Japan's framework is the constructive long-term signal: institutional custody, ETF structures, clearing access. Russia's USDC and BNB commissions create friction for stablecoin adoption in hostile environments. CLARITY Act at 60% means US institutional crypto expansion timelines extend by at least a year.
items.tweetA: Japan is about to regulate crypto like stocks. Effective 2027. That means institutional custody, securities frameworks, ETF structures — the full apparatus. Russia is heading the opposite direction: 2-3% commissions on USDC and BNB from July 1. Two of the world's largest economies, completely opposite frameworks. $BTC $ETH $BNB
items.tweetB: Galaxy cut CLARITY Act 2026-passage odds to 60%. Russia targets USDC and BNB next month. Japan moves toward securities integration. The US is the swing variable in global crypto regulatory convergence. Every month the CLARITY Act slips is a month of institutional custody uncertainty. Watch the Senate calendar.

items.title: Samsung 45K-worker strike concluded — HBM and DRAM supply pressure easing as NDX adds CoreWeave and Astera Labs
items.idea: Samsung's 45,000-worker strike concluded, removing production uncertainty over HBM and DRAM — memory components critical to AI accelerator production. Nasdaq-100 June 22 rebalance adds CoreWeave and Astera Labs, both heavily dependent on HBM availability. Samsung strike had been an underreported supply chain risk for Nvidia delivery timelines.
items.angle: Samsung HBM supply restoration plus CoreWeave and Astera Labs NDX inclusion is a connected story: $300B in passive NDX flows will benchmark against two major HBM consumers on the same week the key input constraint resolves. AI infrastructure supply chain just got less constrained on a precisely timed basis.
items.tweetA: Samsung's 45K-worker strike is over. HBM supply pressure is easing. CoreWeave and Astera Labs join the Nasdaq-100 on June 22. The AI infrastructure supply chain just got less constrained the same week the index that benchmarks $300B in passive flows adds its biggest HBM consumers. $NVDA $CRVW
items.tweetB: The least-covered story in the AI buildout: HBM memory was the bottleneck keeping GPU delivery timelines long. Samsung's strike threatened to keep it that way. The strike ended. HBM supply easing plus two major AI names entering the NDX on June 22 gives the capex execution story one fewer obstacle. $ALAB

items.title: Emerging market CBs retain positive real rates above inflation — structural flexibility DM peers have lost
items.idea: Authers' Z-score analysis of 52 CBs shows EM CBs responded more aggressively, got ahead of the curve earlier, and now retain positive real rates above inflation. DM CBs — ECB hiking into stagflation, Fed facing potential hike into growth headwinds — have lost that flexibility. EM CBs can cut if economies slow; DM peers cannot without reigniting inflation.
items.angle: EM vs DM monetary divergence usually framed as a currency story. Here it is a structural flexibility argument: the places that used to need IMF help are now the ones with optionality. In a world where Fed and ECB face potential hike cycles alongside growth headwinds, EM local currency bonds offer a different risk-reward.
items.tweetA: The global CB split nobody is talking about: EM CBs responded faster to inflation in 2021, got ahead, and now have positive real rates above inflation. DM CBs are hiking from behind, near-zero real rates. Fed and ECB have almost no room to maneuver without recession risk. EM local bonds look different now.
items.tweetB: Authers ran Z-scores on 52 central banks. EM: inflation shock less severe, contained faster, positive real rates retained. DM: massive shock, slow response, reinflating from a worse starting position. The global monetary policy map has flipped. The places with policy optionality have changed sides.

charts.desc: Z-score comparison of EM vs DM inflation shocks and rate paths since 2010 — divergence tells the whole story visually. Rate diffusion index chart. Worth opening Bloomberg.

items.title: Coinbase panel: Bitcoin should prepare for quantum attacks now — no consensus on freezing vulnerable addresses
items.idea: A Coinbase-convened panel concluded Bitcoin should prepare for quantum computing attacks now, but reached no consensus on freezing or invalidating quantum-vulnerable addresses. The threat to Bitcoin's elliptic curve cryptography has been theoretical for years; formal Coinbase panel signals timeline has shortened materially. Also: tokenized SpaceX shares trading on Solana (Sunrise, Backpack Securities) and FIFA World Cup 2026 fan-token volumes elevated on Solana.
items.angle: No consensus on freezing vulnerable coins is the key tension. Most vulnerable are oldest, least-moved coins — potentially including Satoshi's. A protocol freeze would be Bitcoin's most controversial governance decision ever. Lack of consensus means no plan for the most politically toxic question in crypto.
items.tweetA: Coinbase convened a panel on quantum attacks on Bitcoin. Conclusion: prepare now. No consensus on whether to freeze quantum-vulnerable addresses. Translation: the threat is real enough for formal panels, but no plan exists for the most controversial question — what happens to Satoshi's coins? $BTC
items.tweetB: The quantum Bitcoin governance problem has a political economy layer: coins most vulnerable to quantum attacks are the oldest and least-moved. Freezing them means a governance decision touching early miners and Satoshi. No consensus is not a neutral outcome. The Coinbase panel just made the clock more visible.

items.title: 'Compute is the new oil' — Capital Flows announces framework for next stage of macro capital allocation
items.idea: Capital Flows positioning 'Compute is the new oil' as the most mispriced and misunderstood idea in markets. The thesis: compute is becoming the critical bottleneck resource determining economic power, just as oil did in the 20th century — with similar implications for geopolitics, industrial policy, infrastructure investment, and financial flows. Livestream scheduled for tomorrow at 11:30am MST, 1.5 hours before cash equity close.
items.angle: The compute-as-oil framework connects every major thread of 2026: US-Iran war over energy; US-China chip war over compute; AI capex requiring energy for data centers. The person who invests in oil in 1973 invests in compute in 2026. The allocation implications are direct.
items.tweetA: Compute is the new oil. The country controlling the most compute controls the economy. China chip restrictions. TSMC Taiwan position. Nvidia H100 allocation. It is energy policy 1973, but in silicon. The US-Iran war was about oil. The US-China war is about compute. They are the same conflict, different century. $NVDA $TSM
items.tweetB: Every major geopolitical conflict in the 20th century had oil underneath it. 21st century version: compute. The US-Iran war disrupted oil flows. The US-China chip war disrupts compute flows. When capital starts treating them as equivalent strategic resources, you invest differently. Compute is the new oil. The thesis is landing.
